parsed.summary: The v0.4.0→v0.4.6 cumulative diff introduces significant new infrastructure (retry/backoff, error dispatch, job tracking, pre-push hooks) alongside the full deletion of a utility module. Three material gaps deserve attention before this ships as the basis for a v0.4.7 sweep: (1) `process.mjs` was completely removed, risking runtime crashes from any stale import; (2) `glm-client.mjs` has 155 lines changed — the primary API communication layer — with no dedicated test file; (3) v0.4.6's core new feature (BigModel error dispatch + auto retry/backoff) has no UAT report, unlike v0.4.4 and v0.4.5 which both have structured UAT evidence. These are not theoretical risks — they map to the exact failure modes (silent API errors, lost jobs, misrouted retries) that are expensive to detect in production.

findings.title: scripts/lib/process.mjs fully deleted — stale import will crash at runtime
findings.body: The diff stat shows `scripts/lib/process.mjs | 67 ---`, meaning all 67 lines were removed. This was presumably a utility module consumed by other scripts. If any remaining file still imports from `./lib/process.mjs` or `./lib/process`, Node.js will throw MODULE_NOT_FOUND at runtime — and for ESM dynamic imports, this may only surface on the first code path that hits it. Files with large changes that are likely consumers include `glm-companion.mjs` (139 lines changed) and `render.mjs` (79 lines changed). A grep for 'process' imports across the codebase is needed to confirm all call sites were migrated. The risk is a latent crash that only manifests on specific execution paths.
findings.recommendation: Run `grep -r 'process\.mjs\|from.*process' scripts/` to confirm zero remaining imports. If any are found, either migrate them to the replacement module or restore process.mjs with a deprecation shim. Add a CI check that validates all imports resolve (e.g., `node --check` or an import lint).

findings.title: glm-client.mjs has 155 lines changed with no dedicated test file
findings.body: The primary API client module `scripts/lib/glm-client.mjs` has 155 lines of changes (second largest after retry.mjs) yet there is no `tests/glm-client.test.mjs` in the test directory. This module is the communication layer with the external BigModel API — it handles request formatting, response parsing, and presumably now integrates with the new retry and error-dispatch modules. Bugs here can cause silent API failures, malformed requests, dropped responses, or incorrect retry behavior that looks like it succeeds. Every other significant lib module (retry, bigmodel-errors, state, render, fs, args) has a corresponding test file except this one.
findings.recommendation: Create `tests/glm-client.test.mjs` covering at minimum: (a) successful API response parsing, (b) error response classification handoff to bigmodel-errors, (c) retry integration (verify retry is invoked for retryable errors and skipped for non-retryable ones), (d) timeout handling, (e) malformed/empty response handling.

findings.title: v0.4.6 retry + error dispatch shipped without UAT evidence
findings.body: The repository contains structured UAT reports for v0.4.4 (`test-automation/uat-reports/v0.4.4/report.md`) and v0.4.5 (`test-automation/uat-reports/v0.4.5/report.md`), each with scenario JSON files and run scripts. There is no corresponding `test-automation/uat-reports/v0.4.6/` directory. The v0.4.6 commit (`e0a9ba8`) introduces 'BigModel error dispatch + auto retry/backoff' — the most critical new infrastructure in the release — yet this functionality has no end-to-end UAT evidence. Unit tests exist (retry.test.mjs at 352 lines, bigmodel-errors.test.mjs at 189 lines) but these test modules in isolation. The interaction between error classification → retry decision → client behavior → user-visible outcome is untested in an integrated scenario.
findings.recommendation: Create `test-automation/uat-reports/v0.4.6/` with at minimum: (a) a scenario that triggers a retryable API error and verifies the operation eventually succeeds, (b) a scenario that triggers a non-retryable error and verifies it fails immediately with a clear message, (c) a scenario that exhausts retries and verifies graceful degradation. Do not ship v0.4.7 based on v0.4.6 without this evidence.

findings.title: tracked-jobs.mjs modified (78 lines changed) with no test coverage
findings.body: `scripts/lib/tracked-jobs.mjs` has 78 lines of changes — it manages job lifecycle tracking, which is critical for ensuring jobs are not lost, duplicated, or left in an inconsistent state. There is no `tests/tracked-jobs.test.mjs`. Job tracking bugs are particularly insidious because they manifest as: jobs silently disappearing from the tracking list, stale jobs accumulating and never being cleaned up, or race conditions when multiple jobs are submitted concurrently. The state file backing job tracking (managed via `state.mjs`) is a single point of failure for data integrity.
findings.recommendation: Add `tests/tracked-jobs.test.mjs` covering: (a) concurrent job addition/removal, (b) state file corruption recovery, (c) job cleanup on process crash/restart, (d) idempotency of job registration. Consider whether tracked-jobs and state.mjs share a state file and whether partial writes could corrupt it.

next_steps: Run `grep -rn 'from.*process' scripts/` and `grep -rn 'import.*process' scripts/` to confirm no stale references to the deleted process.mjs exist. This is a 5-minute check that eliminates a runtime crash risk.
Create `tests/glm-client.test.mjs` with minimum coverage for error→retry integration paths. This is the highest-value test gap — the client is the one module that touches every API call.

Produce a v0.4.6 UAT report covering retry and error-dispatch scenarios before building v0.4.7 on top of this codebase. The absence of integrated testing for the newest, most complex feature is the strongest reason to pause.

Verify that `scripts/lib/state.mjs` uses atomic writes (write-to-temp + rename) for the state file backing tracked-jobs. Partial writes during crash could corrupt job state. If not using atomic writes, this is a latent data-loss bug.
